title: Bankrupting DoS Attackers
chosen_summary: This study is about Can we make a denial-of-service attacker pay more than the server and honest clients? Consider a model where a server sees a stream of jobs sent by either honest clients or an adversary. The server sets a price for servicing each job with the aid of an estimator, which provides approximate statistical information about the distribution of previously occurring good jobs. We describe and analyze pricing algorithms for the server under different models of synchrony, with total cost parameterized by the accuracy of the estimator. results_summary: Additionally, we prove a lower bound, showing that our pricing algorithm yields asymptotically the same cost as the attacker's cost, even if the attacker has a higher number of good jobs than the server. Finally, the results show that our algorithm can be used to make a denial-of-service attacker pay more than a server and honest clients.
conclusion_summary: Additionally, we prove a lower bound, showing that our pricing algorithm yields asymptotically the same rate of service as the attacker, even if the attacker's cost grows more slowly than the server's cost. title: Finite-Time Accuracy of Temporal-Difference Learning Under Schur-Stable Recursions
chosen_summary: This study is about Finite-Time Accuracy of Temporal-Difference Learning Under Schur-Stable Recursions. Temporal difference (TD) learning is a cornerstone reinforcement learning (RL) method for policy evaluation, where the goal is to estimate the value function of a Markov decision process under a fixed policy. While a substantial body of work has established its convergence and stability properties, more recent efforts have focused on its statistical efficiency through finite-time error bounds. In this study, we advance this line of research by developing a new finite time error analysis for tabular TD learning that directly exploits a discrete-time stochastic linear system representation and leverages Schur stability of the associated matrices. introduction_summary: Vertex-level clustering for directed graphs (digraphs) remains challenging as edge directionality breaks the key assumptions underlying popular spectral methods, which also incur the overhead of eigen-decomposition. this study proposes Parametrized Power-Iteration Clustering (ParPIC), a random-walk-based clustering method for weakly connected digraphs.
methods_summary: Vertex-level clustering for directed graphs (digraphs) remains challenging as edge directionality breaks the key assumptions underlying popular spectral methods, which also incur the overhead of eigen-decomposition. this study proposes Parametrized Power-Iteration Clustering (ParPIC), a random-walk-based clustering method for weakly connected digraphs.
results_summary: this study proposes Parametrized Power-Iteration Clustering (ParPIC), a random-walk-based clustering method for weakly connected digraphs. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world graphs demonstrate that ParPIC achieves competitive clustering accuracy with improved scalability relative to spectral and teleportation-based methods.
conclusion_summary: Vertex-level clustering for directed graphs (digraphs) remains challenging as edge directionality breaks the key assumptions underlying popular spectral methods, which also incur the overhead of eigen-decomposition. This builds over the Power-Iteration Clustering paradigm, which uses the rows of the iterated diffusion operator as a data embedding.

title: Divergence Results and Convergence of a Variance Reduced Version of ADAM
chosen_summary: This study is about ADAM: A Variance Reduction Analysis of ADAM. Despite of its outstanding performance, ADAM has been proved to be divergent for some specific problems. To this end, the authors present a variance reduction assumption, the results show that an ADAM-type algorithm converges, which means that it is the variance of gradients that causes the divergence of original Adam. We revisit the divergent question and provide divergent examples under stronger conditions such as in expectation or high probability.

title: Designing Underactuated Graspers with Dynamically Variable Geometry Using Potential Energy Map Based Analysis
chosen_summary: , this study is about Energy Maps and Tendon-Pulley Gripper Parameter Analysis. Energy maps are used to predict how a part will evolve in the grasp given a specific actuation input to the gripper. These friction-based energy maps were used to evaluate the importance of various tendon-pulley gripper parameters across nearly 6 million simulated grasping scenarios. Specifically, a variable palm width is needed to manipulate parts of varying scales, and a variable transmission ratio, or the ratio of the distal to the proximal pulley radii, is necessary to achieve a caging grasp. These maps were developed using a 3D model of the grasping reaction.

title: A Roadmap for Greater Public Use of Privacy-Sensitive Government Data: Workshop Report
chosen_summary: This study is about A Roadmap for Greater Public Use of Privacy-Sensitive Government Data: Workshop Report. title: Modelleme ve Simulasyon
chosen_summary: This study is about Modelleme ve Simulasyon. Computer modeling and simulation is used to analyze system behavior and evaluate strategies for operating in descriptive or predictive modes. title: FC-PINO: High Precision Physics-Informed Neural Operators via Fourier Continuation
chosen_summary: the physics-informed neural operator (PINO) is a machine learning paradigm that has demonstrated promising results for learning solutions to partial differential equations (PDEs). It leverages the Fourier Neural Operator to learn solution operators in function spaces and leverages physics losses during training to penalize deviations from known physics laws. In this study, the authors present a new type of neural operator that is based on the physics losses of PDEs. The physics losses are caused by the fact that the PDE is a partial differential equation (PDE) that is not directly related to the physical laws of the universe.
